---
title: "Data wrangling and visualization"
subtitle: "INF2167, Week 3"
date: "May 21, 2026"
date-format: long
author: "Inessa De Angelis"
format:
  revealjs:
    pdf-separate-fragments: true
    theme: [default, custom.scss]
    highlight-style: github
    slide-number: true
bibliography: ref.bib
csl: apa.csl
---

## Office Hours
- Reminder that I have weekly office hours: Mondays from 12:00-1:00pm on Zoom
- The Zoom link and passcode are on the syllabus

## Objectives
- Understand key principles of tidy data and why data wrangling is an important step in the research process
- Use core `tidyverse` functions such as `select()`, `filter()`, `mutate()`, and `arrange()` to clean and transform data [@tidyverse]
- Apply the pipe operation (`|>`) to develop readable and reproducible data workflows
- Create basic data visualizations using `ggplot2` to explore and communicate patterns in data
- Critically reflect on how data visualization choices shape interpretation

## Why data wrangling matters
- It is said that **80%** of data analysis is spent on the cleaning and preparing stages [@wickham2014]
- Real-world data is messy and is not ready for analysis the minute we collect it 
- Variables may be inconsistent or incomplete without doing data wrangling 
- The structure of your data and dataset can affect the types of research questions you can realistically ask and answer 

## What is tidy data?
- “Tidy” data is a standard way of mapping the meaning of a dataset to its structure 
- In "tidy" data:

  - Each variable forms a column
  
  - Each observation forms a row
  
  - Each type of observational unit forms a table

## Why is tidy data important?
- Why do you think tidy data is important for doing data science?

## Why tidy data matters
- Easier to analyze

  - Simplifies creating descriptive statistics, visualizations, and statistical modeling
  
- Reduces errors

  - Avoids duplicated values and missing labels
  
  - Easier to spot missing data, inconsistencies, and data quality issues

- Improves reproducibility
  
  - Easier for future us and others to understand and repeat

  - Makes collaboration and version control more reliable

```{r}
#| echo: false
library(here) 
library(tidyverse)
mps_twitter <- read_csv(here("slides/mps_twitter.csv"), show_col_types = FALSE)
```

## Core `tidyverse` functions for data tidying and wrangling
- The `tidyverse` is a package of packages, which means when we install the `tidyverse`, we actually install a bunch of different packages [@alexander2023] 
- The key packages in the `tidyverse` for tidying and manipulating data are `tidyr` [@tidyr] and `dplyr` [@dplyr]
- We are going to focus on few functions such as `pivot_longer`, `select()`, `filter()`, `mutate()`, and `arrange()` today, but this week's assigned chapters from *Modern Dive* and *Telling Stories with Data* (plus Appendix A assigned in Week 1) cover additional functions 

## Quick reminder: The pipe (|>) operator
- Remember from Week 1 that the pipe operator (`|>`) allows us to link together multiple functions, passing the result of one function as the input to the next
- We read it as "then" or "and then"
- You may see the pipe operator also written as "`%>%`" in older code. `%>%` works exactly the same, but it is recommended that you use the newer and more streamlined version (`|>`)

## Example: The pipe
Take the hypothetical sequence of functions:

```bash
x |> 
  f() |> 
  g() |> 
  h()
```
We'd read it as:

- Take `x` then

- Use this output as the input to the next function `f()` then

- Use this output as the input to the next function `g()` then

- Use this output as the input to the next function `h()`

Note that we do not include "`|>`" after `h()` on line 4 because there are no subsequent functions. If you were to include "`|>`" on line 4, you would receive an error message and your code would not run

## Dataset example
- Here's a sample dataset looking at the number of tweets three MPs share each month
- Is this a messy or tidy dataset and why?

```{r}
head(mps_twitter)
```

## Dataset example (continued)
- This is a messy dataset because each month is its own separate column, meaning our “month” variable is spread across columns
- This makes it harder to filter number of tweets by month and summarize trends over time 
- This example illustrates that the structure of our dataset can shape our analysis

## Tidying a dataset
- To tidy the example dataset we just saw, we can use the `pivot_longer` function from `tidyr` (part of the `tidyverse`) to "tidy" the data frame to a "long" format
- If you want to “tidy” the data frame to a “wide” format, you can use `pivot_wider()`

## Tidying a dataset (continued)
To make this a tidy dataset, we could re-shape our dataset so "month" and "number of tweets" become their own columns
```r{}
mps_twitter |> 
 pivot_longer(cols = c(Tweets_Jan, Tweets_Feb, Tweets_Mar),
              names_to = "Month", 
              values_to = "Tweets", 
              names_prefix = "Tweets_")
```
Each row is now one observation and each column is one variable
```{r}
mps_twitter |> 
 pivot_longer(cols = c(Tweets_Jan, Tweets_Feb, Tweets_Mar),
              names_to = "Month", 
              values_to = "Tweets", 
              names_prefix = "Tweets_")
```

## Wide vs long data: why it matters
- **Wide data:** each variable is stored in separate columns

  - Example: Tweets_Jan, Tweets_Feb, Tweets_Mar

- **Long data:** variables are stored in one column and their values in another

  - Example: month + tweets columns
  
- `ggplot2` is designed for long (tidy) data, where each row = one observation and each column = one variable

## select()
- We use `select()` to pick particular columns of a dataset
- This function is useful if you want your dataset to only have a subset of variables/columns
- Let's try using the `select()` function now with data from the `canlang` package which looks at languages spoken across Canada [@canlang]

## Example: select()
Let's load our R packages:
```{r}
#| echo: true
library(tidyverse)
library(canlang)
```

And then load the dataset and select 3 of the 6 columns/variables

```r{}
can_lang |>
select(category, language, mother_tongue)
```

So now our dataset has 3 of the 6 columns/variables

```{r}
can_lang |>
select(category, language, mother_tongue)
```

## Example: select() (continued)
We can also use `select()` to remove columns, by negating the column name (using the minus sign, `-`)

```r{}
can_lang |>
select(-mother_tongue)
```

So now our dataset has 5 of the 6 columns/variables

```{r}
can_lang |>
select(-mother_tongue)
```

## Example: select() (continued)
We specify multiple columns to remove using the minus sign, in conjunction with `c()`

```r{}
can_lang |>
select(-c(mother_tongue, lang_known))
```

So now our dataset has 4 of the 6 columns/variables

```{r}
can_lang |>
select(-c(mother_tongue, lang_known))
```

## Example: select() (continued)
Furthermore, we can use `select()` to only include columns that meet a certain condition, such as all of the columns that start with "most"

```r{}
can_lang |>
select(starts_with("most"))
```

So now our dataset just has the two columns/variables that start with the word "most"

```{r}
can_lang |>
select(starts_with("most"))
```

## filter()
- We use `filter()` to pick out a subset of rows in a dataset
- This function is useful because it helps you narrow down the scope of your dataset to include just the observations you care about

## Example: filter()

We can use `filter()` to pick particular rows of a dataset. For example, we might only be interested in analyzing Indigenous languages

```r{}
can_lang |>
filter(category == "Aboriginal languages")
```
So now our dataset just includes Indigenous languages

```{r}
can_lang |>
filter(category == "Aboriginal languages")
```

## Example: filter() (continued)
We could also use `filter()` with two conditions. Either `&` or `,` render the same result

```r{}
can_lang |>
filter(category == "Aboriginal languages" & language == "Cree, n.o.s.")
```
So now our dataset just includes Cree for Indigenous languages

```{r}
can_lang |>
filter(category == "Aboriginal languages" & language == "Cree, n.o.s.")
```

## Example: filter() (continued)
Moreover, we could combine `filter()` and `select()` by using the pipe operator

```r{}
can_lang |>
filter(category == "Official languages") |>
select(category, language, mother_tongue)
```

Now, our dataset just includes the official language category, the name of the language, and the number of Canadian residents who reported the language as their mother tongue

```{r}
can_lang |>
filter(category == "Official languages") |>
select(category, language, mother_tongue)
```

## arrange()
- We use `arrange()` to change the order of the dataset based on the values of particular columns
- `arrange()` always returns rows sorted in ascending order by default
- To change from ascending to descending order, we can use `desc()` with `arrange()`

## Example: arrange()
For example, we could arrange the mother tongue column by the number of speakers

```r{}
can_lang |>
arrange(mother_tongue) 
```
So now the smallest number of people who have a certain language as their mother tongue appear first in our dataset

```{r}
can_lang |>
arrange(mother_tongue) 
```

## Example: arrange() (continued)
And we can add `desc()` with `arrange()`
```r{}
can_lang |>
arrange(desc(mother_tongue))
```
Now, the largest number of people who have a certain language as their mother tongue appear first in our dataset
```{r}
can_lang |>
arrange(desc(mother_tongue))
```

## mutate()
- Another common transformation of data is to create new variables based on existing ones 
- We can use the `mutate()` function to help us when we want to make a new column in our dataset
- By default, the new column will be added at the *end* of our dataset
- If we want the new column to appear at a particular place in our dataset, we can specify `.before` or `.after` a specific column

## Example: mutate()
Let's say that we want to find out the number of people who use the language at home (most_at_home) and add the number who use it at work (most_at_work). We can do this by creating a new column (total_use):

```r{}
can_lang |>
mutate(total_use = most_at_home + most_at_work) 
```

Instead of looking at two separate variables, we now have one summary variable 

```{r}
can_lang |>
mutate(total_use = most_at_home + most_at_work) |>
  select(-lang_known)
```

## Example: mutate() (continued)
- Let's say we want our new "total_use" column to appear before our columns that start with "most". We can achieve that by using "`.before`"

```r{}
can_lang |>
mutate(total_use = most_at_home + most_at_work, .before = "most_at_home") 
```

Our column now appears at a specific point in our dataset

```{r}
can_lang |>
mutate(total_use = most_at_home + most_at_work, .before = "most_at_home") |>
  select(-lang_known)
```

## summarize()
- We use `summarize()` to calculate descriptive statistics  
- We often use the `group_by()` function in conjunction with `summarize()`
- `summarise()` and `summarize()` are equivalent and we can use either
- We'll talk more about descriptive statistics in Week 6

## Example: summarize()
We want to find the total number of speakers by broad language category. We can achieve this through combining `group_by` and `summarize`
```r{}
can_lang |>
group_by(category) |>
summarize(total_speakers = sum(mother_tongue))
```
This groups the data by category and then calculates the total number of speakers within each group
```{r}
can_lang |>
group_by(category) |>
summarize(total_speakers = sum(mother_tongue))
```

## rename()
- We use `rename()` to change the name of variables/columns in our dataset 
- We use a single equals sign ("=") within the `rename()` because we want to assign a new variable that has the same values as the old variable and then delete the original variable

## rename() (continued)
- The new variable name appears on the left hand side of the equals sign (=), while our old variable name appears on the right

```r{}
can_lang |>
rename(language_known = lang_known)
```
Now our `lang_known` column is called `language_known`

```{r}
can_lang |>
rename(language_known = lang_known) |>
  select(-c(most_at_home, most_at_work))
```


## Data types and structure matter
- **Numeric (quantitative)**  
  - Represent measurable quantities  
  - Can be *continuous* (e.g., life expectancy) or *discrete* (e.g., number of tweets)

- **Character (text)**  
  - Labels or strings (e.g., country names, language names)  
  - Usually not used directly in calculations

## Data types and structure matter (continued)
- **Factor (categorical)**  
  - Variables that represent groups or categories  
  - Can be *unordered* (e.g., gender) or *ordered* (e.g., low / medium / high)

```r{}
str(can_lang)
```

- We can check variable types using `str()`:

```{r}
str(can_lang)
```

## Data types and structure matter (continued)

```r{}
class(can_lang$mother_tongue)
```

- Or we can look at a specific variable in a dataset using `class()`, specifically `class(dataset_name$variable_of_interest)`

```{r}
class(can_lang$mother_tongue)
```

- The type of variable affects how R treats it in:
  - summaries (`summarize()`)
  - grouping (`group_by()`)
  - plots (`ggplot()`)
  
## Why visualize our data?
- Why should we visualize our data?

## Data visualization
- The purpose of a visualization is to answer a question about a dataset of interest [@timbers2022]
- Visualization is an important way understand our data and to communicate this to the reader
- Plotting the observations in a dataset can help us identify outliers, distributions of individual variables, relationships between groups of variables, and missing data 
- An important objective of a graph is to convey as much of the actual data and its context as possible

## How to think about data visualization
- The first thing to do before creating a visualization is to think about the question you are trying to answer
- A good visualization will clearly answer your question without distraction
- But a great visualization will suggest even what the question was itself without additional explanation
- Visualizations are not neutral though and our choices can impact the communication of and understanding of findings

## A very brief history of data visualization
-  @vanderplas2020 highlight that visual abstractions of data goes as far back as sixth-century-BCE Babylon, however the creation of graphs and charts have been linked to the more recent development of coordinate systems
- Data analysis and graphs started becoming more common and appearing in government-issued reports in eighteenth and nineteenth centuries
- Early experimentation regarding the accuracy of graphical forms often came from psychophysics research - the aim focuses on the initial perception and graph comprehension

## Introducing ggplot2

- `ggplot2` is part of the tidyverse and follows a “grammar of graphics”  
-  Similar to linguistic grammar, “the grammar of graphics” defines a set of rules for constructing statistical graphics by combining different types of layers [@moderndive]
- There are a variety of graph types we'll work with, including bar charts, scatterplots, line plots, and histograms

## Essential compontents of graphics
According to @moderndive, essential components of graphics include:

- `data`: the dataset containing the variables of interest

- `geom`: the geometric object in question. This refers to the type of object we can observe in a plot. For example: points, lines, and bars

- `aes`: aesthetic attributes of the geometric object and are mapped to variables in the dataset. For example, x/y position, colour, shape, and size

## Common types of plots
- Common types of plots include: 

  - Bar charts
  - Scatterplots
  - Line plots
  - Histograms
  - Boxplots

## A word on pie charts
- While pie charts are often commonly used, they poorly represent data
- Humans are poor at comparing angles and areas, so pie charts make it difficult to accurately judge differences between categories
- They quickly become cluttered and hard to read when there are many categories

## Bar charts
- We typically use a bar chart when we have a categorical variable that we want to visualize
- They are effective for visualizing comparisons of amounts
- The geometric object called a “geom” that we primarily use is `geom_bar()` but we can also use `geom_col()`
- Hint: `geom_bar()` counts data automatically, while `geom_col()` uses pre-summarized values

## Example: Bar charts
- For example, we may want to visualize the number of interruptions by year in the Australian Parliamentary debates [@katz2026]

![](n_interject_per_yr.jpeg){fig-align="center"}

## Interpreting bar charts

- **x-axis**: year
- **y-axis**: number of interruptions 
- Each bar represents the total number of interjections recorded in a given year (regardless of MP gender or political party affiliation)

![](n_interject_per_yr.jpeg){fig-align="center"}

## Building bar charts

```{r}
#| echo: false
library(here) 
interjections_by_year <- read_csv(here("slides/interjections_by_year.csv"))
```

In this example, our dataset, called "`interjections_by_year`" has already been read in using `read_csv`

```r{}
interjections_by_year |> # use our interjections dataset
  ggplot(aes(x = year, y = n)) + #  x-axis = year, y-axis = number of interjections
  geom_col() + # specify column/graph type
  theme_minimal() + # simple theme without gridlines/background
  labs( # specify labels
    x = "Year", # label for x-axis
    y = "Number of interjections", # label for y-axis
    title = "Interjections in the Australian House of Representatives from 1998-2025") 
    # graph title
```

## Building bar charts (continued)

```{r}
interjections_by_year |>
  ggplot(aes(x = year, y = n)) +
  geom_col() +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs( 
    x = "Year",
    y = "Number of interjections",
    title = "Interjections in the Australian House of Representatives from 1998-2025")
```

## Building bar charts (continued)
- We can also update the number of ticks on our x and/or y-axes using `scale_x_continuous()` or `scale_y_continuous()`

```r{}
interjections_by_year |> 
  ggplot(aes(x = year, y = n)) + 
  geom_col() + 
  theme_minimal() +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = scales::pretty_breaks(n = 16)) +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = scales::pretty_breaks(n = 6)) +
  labs( 
    x = "Year", 
    y = "Number of interjections", 
    title = "Interjections in the Australian House of Representatives from 1998-2025") 
```

## Building bar charts (continued)

```{r}
interjections_by_year |>
  ggplot(aes(x = year, y = n)) +
  geom_col() +
  theme_minimal() +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = scales::pretty_breaks(n = 16)) +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = scales::pretty_breaks(n = 6)) +
  labs( 
    x = "Year",
    y = "Number of interjections",
    title = "Interjections in the Australian House of Representatives from 1998-2025")
```

## Building bar charts (continued)
- We may also like to consider various groupings of the data to generate different insights. For example, we can use colour to look at interruptions by MP gender
- In the code we just saw, we would add `fill`, so the line would be: `ggplot(aes(x = year, y = n, fill = gender))` 

![](n_interject_per_yr_gender.jpeg){fig-align="center"}

## Scatterplots
- Scatterplots can be used to show the relationship between two numeric or continuous variables
- They are effective for visualizing the relationship between two quantitative variables
- We use `geom_point()` to create scatterplots

```{r}
#| echo: false
library(gapminder)
```

## Example: scatterplots 

We can use data from the `gapminder` package [@bryan2025] to look at GDP per capita versus life expectancy

```r{}
ggplot(gapminder, aes(x = gdpPercap, y = lifeExp)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5) +
  labs(x = "GDP per Capita (US Dollars)", y = "Life Expectancy (Years)",
      caption = "Data source: Gapminder")
```

This graph shows the relationship between wealth and health globally 
```{r}
#| fig-align: center

ggplot(gapminder, aes(x = gdpPercap, y = lifeExp)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5) +
  labs(x = "GDP per Capita (US Dollars)", y = "Life Expectancy (Years)", 
       caption = "Data source: Gapminder")
```

## Line plots
-  Line plots show the relationship between two numerical variables
- They are effective for visualizing trends with an independent, ordered quantity

  - Commonly used to show some sense of time on the x-axis: hours, days, weeks, years, etc. 

- Since time is sequential, we can connect consecutive observations of the variable on the y-axis with a line 
- We use `geom_line()` to create line plots

## Example: line plots 

We can look at Canadian life expectancy between 1952 and 2007  

```r{}
gapminder |>
  filter(country == "Canada") |>
  ggplot(aes(x = year, y = lifeExp)) +
  geom_line() +
  labs(x = "Year", y = "Life Expectancy (Years)", caption = "Data source: Gapminder")
```
This graph shows how life expectancy changes over time in Canada

```{r}
#| fig-align: center

gapminder |>
  filter(country == "Canada") |>
  ggplot(aes(x = year, y = lifeExp)) +
  geom_line() +
  labs(x = "Year", y = "Life Expectancy (Years)", caption = "Data source: Gapminder")
```

## Histograms
- Histograms are useful to show the shape of the distribution of a continuous variable
- The full range of the data values is split into intervals called “bins” and the histogram counts how many observations fall into each bin
- We use `geom_histogram()` to create histograms

## Example: histograms

Histograms can be useful to see how life expectancy is distributed globally

```r{}
ggplot(gapminder, aes(x = lifeExp)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 5) +
  labs(x = "Life Expectancy (Years)", y = "Number of Observations (Country-Year Cases)", 
      caption = "Data source: Gapminder")
```
Our graph suggests that many countries have experienced health improvements over time

```{r}
#| fig-align: center

ggplot(gapminder, aes(x = lifeExp)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 5) +
  labs(x = "Life Expectancy (Years)", y = "Number of Observations (Country-Year Cases)", 
      caption = "Data source: Gapminder")
```

## Boxplots
- Boxplots are constructed from the information provided in the five-number summary of a numerical variable
- They typically show five aspects: 1) the median, 2) the 25th, and 3) 75th percentiles. The fourth and fifth elements differ depending on specifics
- We use `geom_boxplot()` to create boxplots

## Example: boxplots

Boxplots can compare how life expectancy differs across continents in a single summary graph

```r{}
ggplot(gapminder, aes(x = continent, y = lifeExp)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  labs(x = "Continent", y = "Life Expectancy (Years)", caption = "Data source: Gapminder")
```

Our graph suggests that there are differences in typical life expectancy across continents

```{r}
#| fig-align: center

ggplot(gapminder, aes(x = continent, y = lifeExp)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  labs(x = "Continent", y = "Life Expectancy (Years)", caption = "Data source: Gapminder")
```

## Themes
- Themes help us define the visual identity of our graph and clearly communicate our findings to our readers
- There are various themes that are built into `ggplot2`, including: `theme_bw()`, `theme_classic()`, `theme_dark()`, and `theme_minimal()`
- The `ggplot2` [cheat sheet](https://github.com/rstudio/cheatsheets/blob/main/data-visualization.pdf) has a full list of themes
- We can use themes by adding them as a layer

## Themes (continued)

![](n_interject_themes.png){fig-align="center"}

## Facets
- When we want to show variation among many variables, we can use facets 
- Faceting will create multiple copies of the same type of plot with matching x and y axes, but whose content will differ
- We use the `facet_wrap()` function to achieve this

## Facets (continued)

- Here's an example of a graph that uses facets to show who is asking the question during debates and the main policy issue the question discusses [@mckay2026]

![](ld_facet.jpg){fig-align="center"}

## Facets (continued)
- Facets are used in this graph to show the type of account and verified variables, while the fill is the politician [@inessa]
- The x-axis is whether a reply contains harassment (binary; yes/no) and the y-axis is the estimated probability that a reply contains harassment (between 0 and 1)

![](tt_facet.jpeg){fig-align="center"}


## Bringing together multiple graphs
- Depending on your dataset, facet wrapping may not be the best way to communicate your results
- Instead, you may want to make multiple separate graphs and then bring them together
- We can do this using the `patchwork` package [@patchwork]

## Patchwork example
```r{}
## Make B&W theme graph ##
theme_bw <- interjections_by_year |>
  ggplot(aes(x = year, y = n)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#B0AFFF") +
  theme_bw() +
  labs(x = "Year", y = "Number of interjections", caption = "theme_bw()")

## Make classic theme graph ##
theme_classic <- interjections_by_year |>
  ggplot(aes(x = year, y = n)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#B0AFFF") +
  theme_classic() +
  labs(x = "Year", y = "Number of interjections", caption = "theme_classic()")

## Make dark theme graph ##
theme_dark <- interjections_by_year |>
  ggplot(aes(x = year, y = n)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#B0AFFF") +
  theme_dark() +
  labs(x = "Year", y = "Number of interjections", caption = "theme_dark()")

## Make minimal theme graph ##
theme_minimal <- interjections_by_year |>
  ggplot(aes(x = year, y = n)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#B0AFFF") +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(x = "Year", y = "Number of interjections", caption = "theme_minimal()")

## Bring all graphs together into one ##
theme_minimal + theme_classic + theme_bw + theme_dark
```

## Patchwork example (continued)
```{r}
library(patchwork)
theme_bw <- interjections_by_year |>
  ggplot(aes(x = year, y = n)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#B0AFFF") +
  theme_bw() +
  labs(x = "Year", y = "Number of interjections", caption = "theme_bw()")

theme_classic <- interjections_by_year |>
  ggplot(aes(x = year, y = n)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#B0AFFF") +
  theme_classic() +
  labs(x = "Year", y = "Number of interjections", caption = "theme_classic()")

theme_dark <- interjections_by_year |>
  ggplot(aes(x = year, y = n)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#B0AFFF") +
  theme_dark() +
  labs(x = "Year", y = "Number of interjections", caption = "theme_dark()")

theme_minimal <- interjections_by_year |>
  ggplot(aes(x = year, y = n)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#B0AFFF") +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(x = "Year", y = "Number of interjections", caption = "theme_minimal()")

theme_minimal + theme_classic + theme_bw + theme_dark
```

## Colours
- Colours are another way to help communicate your findings, but they should not be added unnecessarily
- Colour should increase the amount of information that is communicated or help to distinguish different groups and their relationship

## Colours (continued)
- We can see here that using the political party colours can help readers more easily distinguish how many MPs from each party have Bluesky accounts

![](mps_colour.jpeg){fig-align="center"}

## Colours (continued)

- There are many different ways to change the colours
- Many palettes are available from `RColorBrewer` [@neuwirth2022] can be specified using `scale_fill_brewer()`
- We can also set our own colours, by specifying colour names or hex codes using `scale_color_manual()`, for example:

```bash
scale_color_manual(values=c("#66CC99", "#B0AFFF"))
```

- The `fill` aesthetic corresponds to the colour used to fill the bars, while the `color` aesthetic corresponds to the colour of the outline of the bars

## Colours (continued)

- When using colours, we need to be conscious of contrast and accessibility

- `viridis` is particularly focused on colour-blind palettes and we can specify the palettes using `scale_fill_viridis_d()`

![*Source: Vanderplas et al., 2020*](colour_scale.jpg){fig-align="center"}

## Colours (continued)
- Are the colours in this graph accessible and do they clearly communicate our results?

![](n_interject_bad.png){fig-align="center"}

## Colours (continued)
- Are there other aspects of the graph (such as labels and titles) that could be clarified?

![](n_interject_bad.png){fig-align="center"}

## Visualizations are not neutral
- Choices about scales, colours, and categories shape how we interpret data
- What we include, exclude, or aggregate changes the story a graph can tell
- Visualizations can clarify patterns, but they can also mislead depending on design choices
- Visualizing data involves editorial choices: some are highlighted and others are obscured. These choices have framing effects, which means that they have an impact on people interpret graphics and the information they take away [@datafeminism]

## Visualizations are not neutral (continued)
- @datafeminism argue that all forms of data and knowledge, including data visualizations are situated
- This means they are produced by specific people in specific contexts (cultural, historical, political, and geographic)
- As a result, data visualizations reflect particular perspectives rather than a universal or "objective" view of reality

## Your turn

-   Go to the class GitHub repository, <https://github.com/inessadeangelis/INF2167> and to the "Week 3" folder
-   Download the lab questions called `week3_lab.qmd` that are in that folder

## Submission

-   Please upload your .qmd file and PDF to Quercus under "Assignments" -\> "In-class Lab 3"

## For next week

-   *Telling Stories*, Chapters 6 & 10
-   *Modern Dive*, Chapter 7
-   D’Ignazio, C., & Klein, L. (2020). 6. “The Numbers Don’t Speak for Themselves.” In *Data Feminism*. <https://data-feminism.mitpress.mit.edu/pub/czq9dfs5/release/3>

**And then please read at least 1 of the following:**

- Karstens et al. (2023) 

- Katz & Alexander (2023)

- Stephenson et al. (2021)

## References
